Ferrets need a minimum of 4 hours of free-roaming time outside their cage every single day in a thoroughly ferret-proofed room; kept caged all day, they develop real physical and behavioral problems.
Ferrets can squeeze through gaps wider than just 1 inch, so ferret-proofing means blocking every doorway gap, securing electrical cords, and covering open toilets and recliner mechanisms.

As obligate carnivores, ferrets need a high-protein, high-fat, zero-carbohydrate diet — plant carbohydrates are strongly associated with insulinoma (a pancreatic cancer) in ferrets, so fruits, vegetables, and grains should be avoided entirely.

Pet ferrets typically live 6 to 10 years, and by age 4 to 5 most develop at least one of three common diseases: insulinoma, adrenal gland disease, or lymphoma — annual bloodwork from age 2-3 helps catch these early.

quiz.question: What is the frenzied hopping, bouncing, sideways-leaping display ferrets do when excited called?
How much free-roam time outside their cage do ferrets need every day, at minimum?

name: Guinea Pig
tagline: The vocal, social little veggie lover that needs a friend!
facts: Guinea pigs have over 11 different vocalizations — they 'wheek' when excited about food and 'purr' when content, similar to cats but much louder.
Guinea pigs are highly social herd animals and should almost always be kept in pairs or small groups, since a solo guinea pig becomes anxious and understimulated.
Guinea pigs 'popcorn' — leaping straight up and twisting mid-air — when they're overjoyed.
Guinea pigs cannot make their own Vitamin C, so they need a reliable daily source like fresh bell peppers or leafy greens to avoid scurvy.

Guinea pigs should never be housed on wire-bottom flooring or with cedar/pine bedding — wire causes painful pressure sores (bumblefoot) and cedar/pine oils are toxic and cause respiratory and liver problems.

quiz.question: Why do guinea pigs 'wheek'?
What essential nutrient can guinea pigs not produce on their own and must get from food every day?

name: Hamster
tagline: The pouch-stuffing solo escape artist that needs way more space than the pet store box!
facts: A hamster's cheek pouches can stretch back past its shoulders when fully packed with food, holding a volume close to the size of the hamster's own body.
Hamsters need at least 450 square inches of unbroken cage floor space (with 600-800+ preferred) - dramatically more than the small starter kits sold in most pet stores.

Syrian hamsters are solitary and must be housed alone as adults; even littermates that got along as babies will eventually fight, sometimes fatally, once they reach sexual maturity around 8 to 12 weeks old.

Wet tail, a bacterial intestinal infection causing diarrhea and lethargy, can be fatal within 24 to 48 hours without treatment, so it needs same-day exotic vet care rather than a wait-and-see approach.
Syrian hamsters typically live only 2 to 3 years, and dwarf species (Campbell's, Winter White, Roborovski) often live a similar or slightly shorter 1.5 to 3 years.

quiz.question: What is the minimum recommended floor space for a hamster cage?
Why should adult Syrian hamsters never be housed together?
options: They eat too much food when paired
They are solitary and will eventually fight, sometimes fatally
They can't be sexed reliably so pairing is risky
They need twice the bedding depth when paired

name: Hedgehog
tagline: The spiky little explorer that runs on a wheel all night!
facts: When threatened, hedgehogs roll into a tight ball and can make a variety of cute snuffling and hissing sounds - they're surprisingly vocal!
Hedgehogs have between 5,000 and 7,000 spines on their back.
Hedgehogs can run at speeds up to 6 miles per hour.
Pet hedgehogs need a solid exercise wheel 10 to 12 inches across, and will happily run 5 to 8 miles on it in a single night.

African pygmy hedgehogs (the common pet species) must be kept between 72 and 80 degrees F at all times - below 65°F they can slip into a dangerous state of torpor since they aren't true cold-adapted hibernators.

quiz.question: About how far can a pet hedgehog run on its exercise wheel in a single night?
What temperature range must pet hedgehogs be kept at to avoid a dangerous state of torpor?

name: Rabbit
tagline: The gentle, litter-trainable companion that loves to binky!
facts: Rabbits can jump up to 4 feet high and run up to 25 mph. They're surprisingly athletic!
Rabbits purr when they're happy! Unlike cats, they do it by softly grinding their teeth together.
Rabbits are not low-maintenance pets — they need at least 3 hours of exercise outside their enclosure every day.
Wire-bottom cages should never be used for rabbits: wire floors cause painful pressure sores on their feet (hocks) that are difficult to heal.
Hay should make up 80 to 90% of a rabbit's diet, with unlimited access at all times — it's the single most important food for their gut and teeth.

quiz.question: According to the rabbit care guide, what temperature range should a pet rabbit's living space stay within?
Why should rabbits never be housed on wire-bottom cage flooring?
options: It causes painful pressure sores on their feet (hocks)
It's too loud for rabbits to sleep near
It rusts too quickly to be practical
Rabbits are able to chew straight through it

name: Sugar Glider
tagline: The tiny, gliding, social marsupial that needs a colony and lots of enrichment!
facts: Sugar gliders can glide up to 150 feet in the wild using a skin membrane called a patagium, steering with their tails and even making mid-air turns.
A lone sugar glider can suffer profound loneliness, often developing self-mutilation (barbering/biting) and depression — they must be kept in bonded pairs or small groups, never alone.

Sugar glider diets like the BML (Bourbon's Modified Leadbeater's) and TPG (The Pet Glider) formulas are specifically balanced to hit a roughly 2:1 calcium-to-phosphorus ratio, which prevents Metabolic Bone Disease.
With excellent care — proper companionship, a correctly balanced diet, respected nocturnal schedule, and annual exotic vet exams — sugar gliders can live 10 to 15 years in captivity.

quiz.question: About how far can sugar gliders glide in the wild using their patagium membrane?
Why should sugar gliders never be housed alone?
options: They need total solitude to avoid stress
Lone gliders often develop self-mutilation and depression
They are territorial and will fight to the death if paired
They lose their ability to glide without a partner

name: Ball Python
tagline: The gentle noodle that curls into a perfect ball when shy!
facts: Ball pythons can go up to 6 months without eating (though they shouldn't have to) — when scared, they curl into a tight ball to protect their head, which is how they got their name.

Food refusals lasting a few weeks are common and often not a health concern — common triggers include breeding season (roughly October through March, especially in males), pre-shed, or stress from incorrect enclosure temperatures or hides.

Ball pythons need two snug hides, one on the warm side and one on the cool side — a hide the snake can freely rattle around in provides no security, and feeling exposed is the root cause of most feeding refusals in the hobby.
They should only be fed appropriately-sized frozen/thawed rodents offered with feeding tongs, never live prey — live rodents have bitten and injured snakes during feeding attempts.
Females typically reach 4 to 5 feet and males stay smaller at 3 to 3.5 feet, making ball pythons one of the smallest python species and a manageable size for most keepers.

quiz.question: A healthy adult ball python suddenly refuses food for a couple of weeks. What's the most likely explanation?
options: It has a parasite infection and needs antibiotics immediately
Breeding season, an upcoming shed, or stress from incorrect temperatures/hides — all common and usually not serious
Ball pythons naturally only eat once per year
It has permanently stopped growing and no longer needs food

quiz.question: What is the correct way to feed a pet ball python?
options: Live rodents left in the enclosure overnight
Any size rodent, since ball pythons can adjust their jaws to fit anything
Appropriately-sized frozen/thawed rodents offered with feeding tongs, not live prey
Handling and feeding at the same time to build a bonding routine

name: Boa Constrictor
tagline: The powerful, graceful heavyweight of the pet snake world!
facts: Boa constrictors can sense the heartbeat of their prey using heat-sensitive pits. They literally feel your pulse!
Boa constrictors don't kill by suffocating their prey - constriction cuts off blood flow so fast prey faints within seconds.
Well-cared-for boa constrictors routinely live 20 to 30 years in captivity, with some documented individuals reaching 40 years.
Boas should only be fed frozen-and-thawed prey, never live rodents, since live prey can seriously injure even a large constricting snake.
Adult boa constrictors need serious space - a minimum 6x3x3 ft enclosure, with big females (which can top 8 feet) often needing 8x4x4 ft or larger.

quiz.question: What special sense do boa constrictors use to detect their prey's heartbeat?
According to their care guide, how long can a well-cared-for pet boa constrictor typically live?

name: California Kingsnake
tagline: The boldly banded beginner snake that becomes a gem with handling!
facts: Kingsnakes are immune to the venom of rattlesnakes, copperheads, and cottonmouths, and they actively hunt and eat other snakes, including venomous ones — the name 'King' is well earned.
Snakes smell with their tongues! They flick their forked tongue to collect chemical particles from the air, which California kingsnakes do constantly while exploring.
California kingsnakes are determined, persistent escape artists that methodically test every seam, hinge, and gap in their enclosure, so a lockable lid is essential.
Musking — releasing a pungent musk from cloacal glands — is a normal defensive behavior in juvenile and newly acquired California kingsnakes, and it fades with consistent, calm handling.
Adult California kingsnakes typically reach 3 to 4 feet in length and reach full adult size by 2 to 3 years of age.

quiz.question: Why does the California kingsnake earn the name 'King'?
options: It grows larger than any other North American snake
It is immune to venom from species like rattlesnakes and copperheads and actively hunts other snakes
It is the fastest-moving snake species

quiz.question: According to its care guide, what does it mean if a California kingsnake releases a pungent musk when handled?
options: It is seriously ill and needs a vet
It is a normal defensive behavior, common in juveniles and new adults, that fades with calm handling
It is about to shed its skin

name: Corn Snake
tagline: The curious, colorful beginner snake that never stops exploring!
facts: Corn snakes are named for their distinctive belly pattern that resembles Indian corn kernels, not because they're found in corn fields (though they are)!
Snakes smell with their tongues! They flick their forked tongue to collect chemical particles from the air.
Corn snakes are notorious escape artists — they can compress their bodies and squeeze through gaps that seem impossibly small, so every seam and vent in their enclosure needs to be secured.
Corn snakes commonly live 15 to 20 years in captivity, and some individuals live even longer.
Corn snakes are semi-arboreal and like to climb branches and perches in their enclosure, especially when they're young.

quiz.question: Why are corn snakes actually named 'corn' snakes?
options: Because their belly pattern resembles Indian corn kernels
Because they primarily eat corn borer insects
Because they were first discovered in a cornfield in Ohio
Because their scales are the color of ripe corn

quiz.question: According to their care guide, how long do corn snakes typically live in captivity?

name: Hognose Snake
tagline: The drama queen of snakes: all bluff and zero bite!
facts: When threatened, hognose snakes will flatten their neck, hiss, lunge (mouth closed), and if that fails, flip over and play dead complete with open mouth and tongue hanging out!
Snakes smell with their tongues! They flick their forked tongue to collect chemical particles from the air.
Hognose snakes are technically venomous, but their mild rear-fanged venom is used to subdue amphibian prey like toads and is harmless to humans - bites are rare and only mildly irritating at most.
Feed juvenile hognose snakes every 5 to 7 days and adults every 7 to 14 days, always using frozen-and-thawed prey since live rodents can injure the snake.

Western hognose snakes actually prefer drier ambient conditions (40-60% humidity) than many other pet snakes, but still need a humid hide with damp sphagnum moss for shedding, or retained eye-cap shed can become a problem.

quiz.question: What does a hognose snake do when it feels threatened?
options: It flattens its neck, hisses, lunges with a closed mouth, and may flip over to play dead
It sprays venom at the attacker from a distance
It changes color to blend into its surroundings

quiz.question: Are hognose snakes dangerous to humans because of their venom?
options: Yes, their venom is highly dangerous to people
No, they have no venom of any kind
They have a mild rear-fanged venom meant for subduing prey like toads, which is harmless to humans
Their venom is dangerous only if the snake is very old

name: Milk Snake
tagline: The brilliantly banded mimic that wears venomous colors with no venom required!
facts: Milk snakes are harmless, but their red, black, and yellow banding mimics the deadly coral snake - a survival trick called Batesian mimicry.
Snakes smell with their tongues! They flick their forked tongue to collect chemical particles from the air - true of milk snakes too as they navigate and hunt.
Milk snakes are secretive, semi-fossorial animals that spend most of their time hidden, so their enclosures need multiple snug hides to feel secure.
In the wild, milk snakes are ophiophagous (snake-eaters), which is why they should never be housed with other snakes in captivity.
Milk snakes are notorious escape artists that test every seam of their enclosure, so a secure, lockable lid is non-negotiable.

quiz.question: What survival strategy explains a milk snake's bold red, black, and yellow bands?
options: Camouflage to blend into leaf litter
Batesian mimicry - copying the look of the venomous coral snake
A true venom-warning display, since milk snakes are mildly venomous

quiz.question: According to the classic identification rhyme, which color pattern points to a harmless milk snake rather than a venomous coral snake?

name: Box Turtle
tagline: The self-closing, woodland wanderer that can live for over 100 years!
facts: Box turtles have a hinged plastron (lower shell) that closes so tightly that no predator can get in — they're the only turtles that can completely seal themselves inside their shell.
Box turtles can live for over 100 years, making them one of the longest-living pets out there — some wild individuals have been documented living past a century, per the guide's FAQ.

Never take a wild box turtle home as a pet: wild populations are declining from habitat loss and collection, and box turtles have strong homing instincts that make them try to return to their exact home territory.
A healthy box turtle diet is roughly 50% animal protein and 50% plant matter — earthworms are considered the single best protein source, with juveniles needing even more protein (60-70%) to grow.
Adult box turtles need an annual period of hibernation-like dormancy called brumation; skipping it long-term can cause reproductive problems and a shortened lifespan.

quiz.question: What makes a box turtle's shell unique among turtles?
options: It's soft and flexible like a tortoise's
It has a hinged plastron (bottom shell) that lets it seal completely closed
It changes color based on temperature
It's covered in fur for insulation

quiz.question: According to their care guide, about how long can box turtles live?

name: Red-Eared Slider
tagline: The iconic pet turtle that grows much larger than the pet store suggests!
facts: Red-eared sliders are one of the world's most invasive species — released or escaped pets have established wild populations on every continent except Antarctica.
Adult red-eared sliders get big: females typically reach 10 to 12 inches in shell length while males stay smaller at 6 to 8 inches, far larger than the 4-inch hatchlings sold in pet stores.
A single adult female slider needs a tank of 100 gallons or more, based on the standard rule of 10 gallons of water capacity per inch of shell length.
With proper care — clean water, strong UVB, and a good diet — red-eared sliders can live 20 to 40 years in captivity, a multi-decade commitment many owners underestimate.
It's illegal to sell red-eared sliders with shells under 4 inches in the US under federal law, and illegal everywhere in the US to release them into the wild.

quiz.question: How large can an adult female red-eared slider's shell grow?
How long can red-eared sliders live in captivity with proper care?

name: Russian Tortoise
tagline: The compact, hardy tortoise that can live over 50 years with proper care!
facts: Russian tortoises are one of the world's most cold-tolerant tortoise species and can survive sub-zero temperatures in the wild by hibernating (brumating) deep underground for months at a time.
Russian tortoises can live 40 to 80+ years in captivity, with some individuals documented living over 100 years — a truly lifelong commitment.
Brumation (hibernation) is a natural, necessary part of Russian tortoise biology; tortoises prevented from hibernating can develop reproductive problems and a shortened lifespan over time.
Adult Russian tortoises reach just 6 to 10 inches in shell length, making them one of the smaller tortoise species commonly kept as pets.
Russian tortoises are notorious escape artists that burrow and climb constantly, so enclosure walls need to be sunk 12 inches or more underground and stand at least 12 inches high to contain them.

quiz.question: How long can Russian tortoises typically live in captivity?
options: 40 to 80+ years, with some documented over 100

quiz.question: Why do Russian tortoises need to brumate (hibernate) each year?
options: It's purely optional and has no biological purpose
It's a natural part of their biology — skipping it can cause reproductive problems and a shortened lifespan
It helps them grow faster than tortoises that don't hibernate
It's only relevant for tortoises kept outdoors

name: Sulcata Tortoise
tagline: The third-largest tortoise: a lifetime commitment that may outlive you!
facts: Sulcata tortoises (African spurred tortoises) are the third-largest tortoise species on Earth — a hatchling the size of a ping-pong ball can grow to over 100 lbs and 3 feet long.
Adults commonly reach 24 to 36 inches in shell length and 80 to 150+ lbs, with some exceptional males exceeding 200 lbs.
Sulcatas can live 70 to 150+ years, meaning they will almost certainly outlive their original owner.
About 80% of a sulcata's diet should be grass (Bermuda, orchard, or Timothy hay) with edible weeds and leafy greens as a supplement — fruit and animal protein should be avoided.
Shell pyramiding (raised, bumpy scutes) in sulcatas is caused by excess protein or fruit, too-rapid growth, and low humidity during the growth phase, and once it occurs it is irreversible.

quiz.question: About how big does an adult sulcata tortoise typically get?
What should make up roughly 80% of a sulcata tortoise's diet?
options: Grass (like Bermuda, orchard, or Timothy hay)
